title: Piano

infobox.value: is a [keyboard instrument](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument") that produces sound when its keys are depressed, activating an [action](/wiki/Action_(music) "Action (music)") mechanism where hammers strike [strings](/wiki/String_(music) "String (music)"). Modern pianos have a row of 88 black and white keys—with the exception of the [Bosendörfer](/wiki/B%C3%B6sendorfer "Bösendorfer") and [Stuart & Sons](/wiki/Stuart_%26_Sons "Stuart & Sons") pianos—and tuned to a [chromatic scale](/wiki/Chromatic_scale "Chromatic scale") in [equal temperament](/wiki/Equal_temperament "Equal temperament"). A [musician](/wiki/Musician "Musician") who specializes in piano is called a [pianist](/wiki/Pianist "Pianist").

("harpsichord with soft and loud").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERipinPollens2001a¶2-5) Variations in volume ([loudness](/wiki/Loudness "Loudness")) are produced in response to the pianist's *touch* (pressure on the keys): the greater the pressure, the greater the force of the hammer hitting the strings and the louder the sound produced and the stronger the *attack*. Invented in 1700, the [fortepiano](/wiki/Fortepiano "Fortepiano") was the second keyboard instrument (the first being the [clavichord](/wiki/Clavichord "Clavichord")) to allow gradations of volume and [tone](/wiki/Musical_tone "Musical tone") according to how forcefully or softly the player presses or strikes the keys, unlike the pipe organ and harpsichord.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

A **piano** is a [keyboard instrument](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument") that produces sound when its keys are depressed, activating an [action](/wiki/Action_(music) "Action (music)") mechanism where hammers strike [strings](/wiki/String_(music) "String (music)"). Modern pianos have a row of 88 black and white keys—with the exception of the [Bosendörfer](/wiki/B%C3%B6sendorfer "Bösendorfer") and [Stuart & Sons](/wiki/Stuart_%26_Sons "Stuart & Sons") pianos—and tuned to a [chromatic scale](/wiki/Chromatic_scale "Chromatic scale") in [equal temperament](/wiki/Equal_temperament "Equal temperament"). A [musician](/wiki/Musician "Musician") who specializes in piano is called a [pianist](/wiki/Pianist "Pianist").

There are two main types of piano: the [grand piano](#Grand) and the [upright piano](#Upright). The grand piano offers better sound and more precise key control, making it the preferred choice when space and budget allow. The grand piano is also considered a necessity in venues hosting skilled pianists. The upright piano is more commonly used because of its smaller size and lower cost.

When a key is depressed, the strings inside are struck by felt-coated wooden hammers. The vibrations are transmitted through a [bridge](/wiki/Bridge_(instrument) "Bridge (instrument)") to a [soundboard](/wiki/Soundboard_(music) "Soundboard (music)") that amplifies the sound by [coupling](/wiki/Coupling_(physics) "Coupling (physics)") the [acoustic energy](/wiki/Sound "Sound") to the air. When the key is released, a damper stops the string's vibration, ending the sound. Most notes have three strings, except for the bass, which graduates from one to two. [Notes](/wiki/Musical_note "Musical note") can be [sustained](/wiki/Envelope_(music) "Envelope (music)") when the keys are released by the use of [pedals](/wiki/Piano_pedals "Piano pedals") at the base of the instrument, which lift the dampers off the strings. The sustain pedal allows pianists to connect and overlay sound, and achieve expressive and colorful sonority.

In the 19th century, influenced by [Romantic music](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music") trends, the [fortepiano](/wiki/Fortepiano "Fortepiano") underwent changes such as the use of a [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") frame, which allowed much greater string tensions. [Aliquot stringing](/wiki/Aliquot_stringing "Aliquot stringing") gave grand pianos a more powerful sound, a longer sustain, and a richer tone. Later in the century, as the piano became more common it allowed families to listen to a newly published musical piece by having a family member play a [simplified version](/wiki/Reduction_(music) "Reduction (music)").

The piano is widely employed in [classical](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [traditional](/wiki/Traditional_music "Traditional music") and [popular](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music") music for [solo](/wiki/Piano_concerto "Piano concerto") and [ensemble](/wiki/Musical_ensemble "Musical ensemble") performances, accompaniment, and for [composing](/wiki/Musical_composition "Musical composition"), [songwriting](/wiki/Songwriting "Songwriting") and rehearsals. Despite its weight and cost, the piano's versatility, the extensive training of musicians, and its availability in venues, schools, and rehearsal spaces have made it a familiar instrument in the Western world.

## History

The piano was based on earlier technological innovations in struck-string instruments and [keyboard instruments](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument"). The earliest known keyboard instrument was the Ancient Greek [hydraulis](/wiki/Hydraulis "Hydraulis"), a type of [pipe organ](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ") invented in the third century BC.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Pipe organs continued to be used in Europe through the middle ages, and as such the development of pipe organs enabled instrument builders to learn about creating keyboard mechanisms for sounding [pitches](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)"). The first [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") with struck strings were the [hammered dulcimers](/wiki/Hammered_dulcimer "Hammered dulcimer"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) which originated in the Middle East and were introduced in Europe in the Middle ages. During the Middle Ages, there were several attempts at creating stringed keyboard instruments with struck strings.[[3]](#cite_note-3) By the 17th century, the mechanisms of keyboard instruments such as the [clavichord](/wiki/Clavichord "Clavichord") and the [harpsichord](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord") were well developed. In a clavichord the strings are struck by [tangents](/wiki/Tangent_(clavichord) "Tangent (clavichord)"), while in a harpsichord they are mechanically plucked by quills when the performer depresses the key. Centuries of work on the mechanism of the harpsichord in particular had shown instrument builders the most effective ways to construct the case, soundboard, bridge, and mechanical action for a keyboard intended to sound strings.

The English word *piano* is a shortened form of the Italian **pianoforte**,[[4]](#cite_note-4) derived from **gravecembalo col piano e forte** ("harpsichord with soft and loud").[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERipinPollens2001a¶2-5) Variations in volume ([loudness](/wiki/Loudness "Loudness")) are produced in response to the pianist's *touch* (pressure on the keys): the greater the pressure, the greater the force of the hammer hitting the strings and the louder the sound produced and the stronger the *attack*. Invented in 1700, the [fortepiano](/wiki/Fortepiano "Fortepiano") was the second keyboard instrument (the first being the [clavichord](/wiki/Clavichord "Clavichord")) to allow gradations of volume and [tone](/wiki/Musical_tone "Musical tone") according to how forcefully or softly the player presses or strikes the keys, unlike the pipe organ and harpsichord.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Invention

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/CristoforiPiano1726LeipzigKeyboardView.jpg/250px-CristoforiPiano1726LeipzigKeyboardView.jpg)](/wiki/File:CristoforiPiano1726LeipzigKeyboardView.jpg)

The 1726 Cristofori piano in the [Musikinstrumenten-Museum](/wiki/Museum_of_Musical_Instruments_of_the_University_of_Leipzig "Museum of Musical Instruments of the University of Leipzig") in Leipzig

The invention of the piano is credited to [Bartolomeo Cristofori](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Cristofori "Bartolomeo Cristofori") of [Padua](/wiki/Padua "Padua"), Italy, who was employed by [Ferdinando de' Medici, Grand Prince of Tuscany](/wiki/Ferdinando_de%27_Medici,_Grand_Prince_of_Tuscany "Ferdinando de' Medici, Grand Prince of Tuscany"), as the Keeper of the Instruments.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Cristofori was an expert harpsichord maker and was well acquainted with the body of knowledge on stringed keyboard instruments. This knowledge of keyboard mechanisms and actions helped him to develop the first pianos. It is not known when Cristofori first built a piano. An inventory made by his employers, the [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici") family, indicates the existence of a piano by 1700. The three Cristofori pianos that survive today date from the 1720s.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERipinPollens2001b¶1-8)[[9]](#cite_note-metmuseum-9) Cristofori named the instrument *un cimbalo di cipresso di piano e forte* ("a keyboard of [cypress](/wiki/Cypress "Cypress") with soft and loud"), abbreviated over time as *pianoforte*, *fortepiano*, and later reduced to only *piano*.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsacoff201223-10)

Cristofori's great success was designing a stringed keyboard instrument in which the notes are struck by a hammer. The hammer must strike the string but not remain in contact with it, because continued contact would [damp](/wiki/Damping_(music) "Damping (music)") the sound and stop the string from vibrating and making sound. This means that after striking the string, the hammer must quickly fall from (or rebound from) the strings. The hammer must return to its rest position without bouncing violently, thus preventing notes from being re-played by accidental rebound. It must return to a position in which it is ready to play again almost immediately after its key is depressed, so the player can repeat the same note rapidly when desired. Cristofori's piano [action](/wiki/Action_(piano) "Action (piano)") was a model for the many approaches to piano actions that followed in the next century.

Cristofori's early instruments were made with thin strings and were much quieter than the modern piano, though they were louder and had more [sustain](/wiki/Sustain "Sustain") compared to the clavichord—the only previous keyboard instrument capable of dynamic nuance responding to the player's touch, the velocity with which the keys are pressed. While the clavichord allows expressive control of volume and sustain, it is relatively quiet even at its loudest. The harpsichord produces a sufficiently loud sound, especially when a coupler joins each key to both [manuals](/wiki/Manual_(music) "Manual (music)") of a two-manual harpsichord, but it offers no dynamic or expressive control over individual notes. The piano in some sense offers the best of both of the older instruments, combining the ability to play at least as loudly as a harpsichord with the ability to continuously vary dynamics by touch.

### Early fortepiano

Main article: [Fortepiano](/wiki/Fortepiano "Fortepiano")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Grand_Piano_1781_France_-_Louis_Bas.jpg/250px-Grand_Piano_1781_France_-_Louis_Bas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Piano_1781_France_-_Louis_Bas.jpg)

A grand piano by Louis Bas of [Villeneuve-lès-Avignon](/wiki/Villeneuve-l%C3%A8s-Avignon "Villeneuve-lès-Avignon"), 1781. Earliest French grand piano known to survive; includes an inverted wrestplank and action derived from the work of Bartolomeo Cristofori (ca. 1700) with ornately decorated soundboard.

Cristofori's new instrument remained relatively unknown until an Italian writer, [Scipione Maffei](/wiki/Scipione_Maffei "Scipione Maffei"), wrote an enthusiastic article about it in 1711, including a diagram of the mechanism, that was translated into German and widely distributed.[[9]](#cite_note-metmuseum-9) Most of the next generation of piano builders started their work based on reading this article. One of these builders was [Gottfried Silbermann](/wiki/Gottfried_Silbermann "Gottfried Silbermann"), better known as an [organ](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)") builder. Silbermann's pianos were virtual copies of Cristofori's, with one important addition: Silbermann invented the forerunner of the modern [sustain pedal](/wiki/Sustain_pedal "Sustain pedal"), which lifts all the dampers from the strings simultaneously.[[11]](#cite_note-Badura-Skoda_2000_1–16-11) This innovation allows the pianist to sustain the notes that they have depressed even after their fingers are no longer pressing down the keys. As such, by holding a chord with the sustain pedal, pianists can relocate their hands to a different register of the keyboard in preparation for a subsequent section.

Silbermann showed [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") one of his early instruments in the 1730s, but Bach did not like the instrument at that time, saying that the higher notes were too soft to allow a full dynamic range. Although this earned him some animosity from Silbermann, the criticism was apparently heeded.[[11]](#cite_note-Badura-Skoda_2000_1–16-11) Bach did approve of a later instrument he saw in 1747 and even served as an agent in selling Silbermann's pianos. "Instrument: piano et forte genandt"—a reference to the instrument's ability to play soft and loud—was an expression that Bach used to help sell the instrument when he was acting as Silbermann's agent in 1749.[[12]](#cite_note-palmieri-12)

Piano making flourished during the late 18th century in the [Viennese school](/wiki/First_Viennese_School "First Viennese School"), which included [Johann Andreas Stein](/wiki/Johann_Andreas_Stein "Johann Andreas Stein") (who worked in [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg"), Germany) and the Viennese makers [Nannette Streicher](/wiki/Nannette_Streicher "Nannette Streicher") (daughter of Stein) and [Anton Walter](/wiki/Anton_Walter "Anton Walter"). Viennese-style pianos were built with wood frames, two strings per note, and leather-covered hammers.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1998b23-13) Some of these Viennese pianos had the opposite coloring of modern-day pianos; the natural keys were black and the accidental keys white.[[14]](#cite_note-vienna-14) It was for such instruments that [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart") composed his [concertos](/wiki/Piano_concerto "Piano concerto") and [sonatas](/wiki/Piano_sonata "Piano sonata"), and replicas of them are built in the 21st century for use in [authentic-instrument performance](/wiki/Historically_informed_performance "Historically informed performance") of his music. The pianos of Mozart's day had a softer tone than modern pianos (those from the late 19th century or later) or English pianos, with less sustaining power. The term *fortepiano* now distinguishes these early instruments (and modern re-creations) from later pianos.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy_et_al.2012"Fortepiano"-15)

In the period from about 1790 to 1860, the Mozart-era piano underwent significant changes that led to the modern structure of the instrument. This revolution was in response to a preference by composers and pianists for a more powerful, sustained piano sound,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHamilton199862-16) which was made possible by the ongoing [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") with resources such as high-quality [piano wire](/wiki/Piano_wire "Piano wire") for strings and precision [casting](/wiki/Metal_casting "Metal casting") for the production of massive [iron frames](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") that could withstand the tremendous tension of the strings.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Over time, the tonal range of the piano was also increased from the five [octaves](/wiki/Octave "Octave") of Mozart's day to the seven octave (or more) range found on today's pianos.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiordano2010118-18)

Early technological progress in the late 18th century owed much to the firm of [Broadwood](/wiki/Broadwood_and_Sons "Broadwood and Sons"). [John Broadwood](/wiki/John_Broadwood "John Broadwood") joined with another Scot, Robert Stodart, and a Dutchman, [Americus Backers](/wiki/Americus_Backers "Americus Backers"), to design a piano in the harpsichord case—the origin of the "grand".[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECole2001¶8-19) This was achieved by about 1777. They quickly gained a reputation for the splendour and powerful tone of their instruments, with Broadwood constructing pianos that were progressively larger, louder, and more robustly constructed.

They sent pianos to both [Joseph Haydn](/wiki/Joseph_Haydn "Joseph Haydn") and [Ludwig van Beethoven](/wiki/Ludwig_van_Beethoven "Ludwig van Beethoven"), and were the first firm to build pianos with a range of more than five octaves: five octaves and a [fifth](/wiki/Perfect_fifth "Perfect fifth") during the 1790s, six octaves by 1810 (Beethoven used the extra notes in his later works), and seven octaves by 1820. The [Viennese](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") makers similarly followed these trends. The two schools used different piano actions: Broadwoods used a more robust action, whereas Viennese instruments were more sensitive.

By the 1820s, the center of piano innovation had shifted to Paris, where the [Pleyel](/wiki/Pleyel_et_Cie "Pleyel et Cie") firm manufactured pianos used by [Frédéric Chopin](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Chopin "Frédéric Chopin"), and the Érard firm manufactured those used by [Franz Liszt](/wiki/Franz_Liszt "Franz Liszt"). In 1821, [Sébastien Érard](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_%C3%89rard "Sébastien Érard") invented the double escapement action, which incorporated a *repetition lever* (also called the *balancier*) that permitted repeating a note even if the key had not return to its resting position.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1998c45-20) This facilitated rapid playing of repeated notes, a musical device exploited by Liszt. When the invention became public, as revised by [Henri Herz](/wiki/Henri_Herz "Henri Herz"), the double escapement action gradually became standard in grand pianos and is still incorporated into all grand pianos currently produced in the 2000s.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1998c45-20)

Other improvements of the mechanism included the use of firm felt hammer coverings instead of layered leather or cotton. Felt, which [Jean-Henri Pape](/wiki/Jean-Henri_Pape "Jean-Henri Pape") was the first to use in pianos in 1826, was a more consistent material, permitting wider dynamic ranges as hammer weights and string tension increased.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1998c44-21) The [sostenuto pedal](/wiki/Sostenuto "Sostenuto") ([see below](#Pedals)), invented in 1844 by [Jean-Louis Boisselot](/wiki/Jean-Louis_Boisselot "Jean-Louis Boisselot") and copied by the [Steinway](/wiki/Steinway_%26_Sons "Steinway & Sons") firm in 1874,[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowland1998c47-22) allowed for a wider range of effects.
